intro: We are Dr. Alex and Dr. Sarah — expert agents trained by Hossein Jamalirad, PhD Candidate from Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Department of Medical Informatics, under the supervision of Dr. Hassan Vakili Arki and Dr. Saeid Eslami from the Medical Informatics group at MUMS, and under clinical supervision of Dr. Marjan Sabbaghian and Associate Clinical Professor Dr. Muhammad Ali Sadighi Gilani from Royan Institute Male Infertility Referral Center in Tehran. In this episode we walk through the System Usability Scale evaluation of the micro-TESE Clinical Decision Support System.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Welcome back, Sarah. This episode focuses on the usability evaluation of the micro-TESE CDSS — specifically the System Usability Scale, or SUS, assessment. The study was titled "System Usability Scale (SUS) Expert Evaluation of the micro-TESE Clinical Decision Support System" and was conducted from October 14-28, 2024.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Right, and this was a mixed-methods expert usability evaluation of the micro-TESE CDSS v2.1 (Web-based PHP application). The system is a web-based clinical decision support tool built on the CatBoost model we discussed in the model performance episode — AUC 0.8306, trained on 2,413 NOA patients from Royan Institute.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The evaluation protocol was structured in four phases. Phase 1 was a 15-minute system orientation using a standardized briefing document. Phase 2 was task-based evaluation with a think-aloud protocol running 45 minutes, covering three clinical scenarios. Phase 3 was the standard 10-item SUS questionnaire — 10 minutes. And Phase 4 was a 20-minute semi-structured qualitative interview.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: The three clinical scenarios were carefully designed to test the full range of the system. Scenario A was a favorable prognosis case — a 28-year-old with hypospermatogenesis, normal FSH, and adequate testicular volumes. Scenario B was intermediate — a 35-year-old with Klinefelter syndrome, elevated FSH, and mixed histopathology. And Scenario C was unfavorable — a 42-year-old with bilateral Sertoli cell-only syndrome, severely elevated FSH, and AZFc microdeletion.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: These scenarios map onto real clinical decision points a urologist would face in the micro-TESE preoperative consultation — from straightforward cases where the model provides reassurance, to complex cases where every input variable matters.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: The SUS scoring follows the standard Brooke 1996 algorithm: 5-point Likert scale from Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree, with odd items scored as contribution equals score minus 1, even items as contribution equals 5 minus score, and the total multiplied by 2.5 to get the final SUS score on a 0-to-100 scale.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: It's worth noting this evaluation was formally classified as a quality improvement and system development activity, exempt from full IRB review per institutional policy. Now let's look at who evaluated the system.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Let's talk about the expert panel.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The panel consisted of 5 expert evaluators. The gender distribution was female and male — specifically 3 female and 2 male experts. Average age was 44.8 years with a range of 39 to 52.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And the experience level was substantial — mean of 17.2 years, ranging from 13 to 24 years. These aren't junior evaluators — they're senior academics and clinicians.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Let me walk through the panel. Expert 1 was Prof. Maria Elena Rossi, a Medical Informatics specialist serving as Professor of Biomedical Informatics and Health Information Systems at Department of Biomedical Informatics, University of Rome La Sapienza, Rome, Italy. Credentials: MD, PhD (Biomedical Informatics).

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Expert 2 was Dr. James Kofi Mensah, specializing in Urology (Male Infertility & Microsurgery), serving as Senior Urologist and Associate Professor at Division of Urology, University of Cape Town and Groote Schuur Hospital, Cape Town, South Africa. With 19 years of experience.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Expert 3 was Dr. Yuki Tanaka, an Andrology and Reproductive Medicine specialist, Associate Professor of Reproductive Medicine at Center for Reproductive Medicine and Andrology, Keio University School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan. 16 years of experience, credentials: MD, PhD (Reproductive Endocrinology).

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Expert 4 was Dr. Henrik Lindqvist, specializing in Healthcare UX and Human Factors Engineering. A Senior Researcher in Healthcare Human-Computer Interaction at Division of Human Factors and Health Informatics, KTH Royal Institute of Technology & Karolinska Institutet, Stockholm, Sweden. 13 years of experience with credentials: MSc (Human-Computer Interaction), PhD (Health Informatics).

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And Expert 5 was Dr. Priya Venkataraman, a Clinical Informatics specialist, Assistant Professor of Clinical Informatics; Practicing Internist at Department of Clinical Informatics and Digital Health, All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS), New Delhi, India. 14 years of experience, credentials: MBBS, MD (Internal Medicine), MS (Clinical Informatics).

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: So the panel covers Medical Informatics, Urology with male infertility focus, Andrology and Reproductive Medicine, Healthcare UX and Human Factors Engineering, and Clinical Informatics. That's a well-rounded multidisciplinary evaluation team. Now let's see how they scored the system.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The headline result: the mean SUS score across all 5 experts was 77.0 out of 100, with a standard deviation of 8.73. The median was 77.5, with scores ranging from 65.0 to 87.5. That's a range of 22.5 points.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And in terms of standardized interpretation — using the Bangor 2009 adjective scale, that maps to "Good" usability. On the Bangor 2008 acceptability scale, it falls in the "Acceptable" range. And using Sauro-Lewis 2016 grading, it's a letter grade "B", placing it in the Top 30% (approximately 70th percentile).

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The 95 percent confidence interval was [66.16, 87.84] with a standard error of 3.91. That CI is wide because of the small sample size — n equals 5 — but the lower bound at 66.16 still sits above the marginal cutoff of 51.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Internal consistency was measured by Cronbach's alpha at 0.783, which is classified as "Acceptable to Good (>=0.70)". For a 10-item scale with only 5 respondents, that's actually quite solid — it means the experts were internally consistent in their assessments.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Now the individual scores are revealing. Prof. Maria Elena Rossi from Medical Informatics scored 77.5, letter grade B, Top 30% (>=70th percentile). Dr. James Kofi Mensah from Urology (Male Infertility & Microsurgery) scored 82.5, letter grade A-, Top 15% (>=85th percentile).

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Dr. Yuki Tanaka from Andrology and Reproductive Medicine gave the highest score at 87.5, letter grade A, Top 10% (>=90th percentile). That's an excellent rating from a reproductive medicine specialist.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The lowest score came from Dr. Henrik Lindqvist, the Healthcare UX and Human Factors Engineering expert, at 65.0, letter grade C-, Bottom 40% (~35th percentile). This makes clinical sense — a UX specialist would naturally identify more interaction-level issues than a domain clinician.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And Dr. Priya Venkataraman from Clinical Informatics scored 72.5, letter grade C, at the Median (~50th percentile). The pattern is clear: clinical domain experts rated higher, UX and integration specialists rated lower, indicating strong clinical utility with identifiable implementation gaps.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Looking at the 10 SUS items individually. Item 1 — 'I think that I would like to use this system frequently' — scored a raw mean of 4 out of 5 with SD 0.71. That's a strong endorsement of regular clinical use.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Item 2 — 'I found the system unnecessarily complex' — scored 1.8 with SD 0.84. Remember this is a negative item, so low scores are good. A mean below 2 means experts generally disagreed that the system was complex.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Item 3 — 'I thought the system was easy to use' — got the highest mean at 4.2 with SD 0.45. Notably the minimum was 4 — no expert rated ease of use below 4.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Item 6 — 'I thought there was too much inconsistency in this system' — is interesting because the SD was 0.0. All five experts gave exactly the same score of 2. That unanimous agreement on minimal inconsistency is striking.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Item 7 — 'I would imagine most people would learn to use this system very quickly' — scored 4.4 with SD 0.55. High learnability perception. And Item 9 — 'I felt very confident using the system' — scored 4 with SD 0.71. User confidence was high.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: The weaker items were Item 4 about needing technical support — mean 2 — and Item 8 about the system being cumbersome — mean 2.2. Both are negative items, and scores around 2 indicate mild disagreement. So some experts felt occasional technical support might be needed, especially for the pathology encoding inputs.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The overall pattern tells a consistent story: high marks on learnability, confidence, and desire to use the system regularly; moderate marks on complexity and technical support needs. The system works well clinically but has refinement opportunities in workflow integration.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Exactly. And this is consistent with the difference between clinical experts scoring higher and UX experts scoring lower — the clinical value is proven, the interaction design has room to improve.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Now the qualitative feedback from the semi-structured interviews. Three main strength themes and three weakness themes emerged from thematic analysis.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Strength theme 1: Clinical relevance and comprehensiveness of predictors. Experts reported strong alignment between system inputs and real-world micro-TESE preoperative workflow, including genetics, hormones, and bilateral histopathology.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Strength theme 2: Transparent and interpretable AI outputs. SHAP explanations and layered outputs (risk category, feature importance, recommendations) improved clinical confidence in model reasoning.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Strength theme 3: Learnability and practical consultation utility. Users valued intuitive layout, color-coded validation, and printable outputs for patient counseling and shared decision-making.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: On the weakness side. Theme 1: Interoperability and deployment constraints. Absence of HL7 FHIR/openEHR integration and terminology mapping limits direct EHR interoperability.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Theme 2: Workflow friction and UI resilience gaps. Manual data entry burden, limited tablet optimization, and lack of autosave/error recovery were recurrent usability pain points.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Theme 3: Governance and external validity concerns. Experts requested model version traceability, audit logging, uncertainty communication, and multi-center external validation.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: The feedback pattern is textbook for a first-generation clinical AI tool — strong domain fit and clinical utility, with infrastructure and integration gaps that are addressable in subsequent development cycles.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Let's wrap up with the conclusions. The expert SUS evaluation indicates good usability and acceptable clinical readiness for supervised deployment after targeted informatics and UX refinements.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Key finding one: Mean SUS score was 77.0 (SD 8.73), mapped to Good usability and Acceptable range.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Key finding two: Internal consistency was acceptable-to-good (Cronbach alpha 0.783).

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Key finding three: Clinical domain experts rated the system higher than UX/integration specialists, indicating strong clinical utility with identifiable implementation gaps.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The recommendations going forward: first, Prioritize interoperability and governance modules before wide-scale deployment.. Second, Maintain explainability-first outputs for clinician trust and patient counseling.. And third, Conduct prospective field usability testing in live clinical workflows..

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: The SUS score of 77.0 — Good usability, Acceptable range, letter grade B — positions this CDSS well for supervised clinical deployment. It's above the industry benchmark of 68, and the clinical domain experts consistently rated it higher, confirming the system's value in real micro-TESE preoperative consultations.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And importantly, the weaknesses identified are all actionable — interoperability standards, UI resilience, and governance features are engineering tasks, not fundamental design flaws. The clinical decision support logic itself was validated by every expert.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: That concludes our SUS usability evaluation episode. This evaluation was conducted as part of Hossein Jamalirad's PhD research at MUMS, under the supervision of Dr. Vakili Arki, Dr. Eslami, Dr. Sabbaghian, and Dr. Sadighi Gilani. This media is generated from this investigation with Hossein Jamalirad's Architecture's agent.
